data.title: Micron Q3 Preview: I Predicted This Surge, And It's Not Too Late
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Micron (NASDAQ:MU), the stock has gained 19% in price.

As investors gear up for Micron's Q3 FY2025 earnings, it’s important to look through the surface optimism to understand the underlying strategic developments taking place. The cycle emerging from ever-increasing memory consumption linked to AI is structurally different from the past. Understanding Micron's distinct positioning in the memory market as the leader in High Bandwidth Memory ("HBM") with massive U.S. capital investment is critical to understanding the stock’s potential. Investors should be acutely focused on management's subtle signals regarding regionally stabilized pricing outlook and inventory build. The upside potential is significant, but cyclical and geopolitical realities will decide whether Micron is a medium-term holding or better as a short-term cyclical play at this time.

Q3 Is Set To Be A Blockbuster Moment

Micron is guiding for a record-setting Q3 FY2025 with projected revenue of $8.8 billion ± $0.2 billion and non-GAAP EPS (earnings per share) of $1.57 ± $0.10. This is a sequential increase over Q2's $8.05 billion, reflecting healthy demand from both AI and the data center market. Management forecasts gross margins at approximately 36.5%, a decline from Q1's ~39% number, due to NAND pricing and product mix.

The NAND market is especially difficult, with an overall industry supply/demand imbalance continuing to negatively impact the market price for memory. Micron is actively addressing challenges with NAND given that it is underutilizing its NAND manufacturing facilities and has announced plans to reduce wafer capacity by over 10% year-over-year through FY2025. Though intended to stabilize pricing, these plans take immediate margin impact. Overall, storage revenue was down 20% in Q2, while mobile revenue was down 30%, as OEMs (original equipment manufacturers) work their way through the excess inventory.

However, in spite of market headwinds, Micron continues to emphasize its strategic investment plan. Capital expenditure ("capex") will continue to be substantial; the guidance is over $3 billion for Q3, and Micron has pointed to overall capex guidance of ~$14 billion for FY2025. This encompasses significant initiatives like a new DRAM manufacturing facility in Idaho, an HBM production facility in Singapore, and a testing center in Taiwan. While none of these will affect output in the short term, they set up Micron for longer-term growth opportunities.

Operating cash flow remains healthy, at $3.9 billion in Q2, and the company has $9.6 billion in cash and marketable securities. Management has reiterated its capital expenditure plans for the coming months, and investors will be looking for more guidance from management during Q3 earnings discussions.

Strategic Catalysts Fuel The Engine For Returns

Micron is positioning itself in the memory space in support of AI, and HBM is at the core of its strategic vision. In Fiscal Q2, HBM revenue crossed $1 billion for the quarter, up over 50% from Q1, and this growth stemmed mainly from demand for AI accelerators, like Nvidia's (NVDA) H100, HGX, GH200/GB200, and AMD's (AMD) MI350. Micron's HBM3E is already designed into both Nvidia's GH200/GB200 and GB300 platforms, and AMD's MI350. Micron's HBM supply is sold out through the end of 2025 and new deals for 2026 are in progress, and management expects the total HBM market to be greater than $35 billion next year. By Q4 FY2025, management expects HBM to reflect a DRAM share consistent with Micron's overall DRAM footprint.

Micron is also advancing the roadmap for HBM4, which is due in 2026. Based on Micron's estimates, HBM4 will offer more than 60% more bandwidth than HBM3E, and given the silicon intensity of HBM (3–4 times the wafer usage relative to DDR5), supply will remain structurally tight, supporting pricing power. In addition to HBM, Micron is also pushing its DRAM roadmap with LPDDR5X, targeted at smartphones and edge AI. The 1β node is already being mass-produced, and Micron is collaborating with Nvidia on a new artificial intelligence-optimized memory form factor (SO-CAMM).

To support long-term leadership, Micron plans a $200 billion expansion in the U.S. over the next decade: $150 billion for manufacturing facilities and $50 billion for R&D, with $6.4 billion in CHIPS Act support secured. Two DRAM fabs are planned in Idaho, with up to four in New York, and domestic HBM packaging expansion in Virginia. This means shifting 40% of DRAM production to the U.S., which will increase supply chain resilience and align with U.S. policy.

Micron Is An Exemplary Portfolio Play Still In Action

When considering Micron's growth horizon (i.e. triple-digit FY25 normalized EPS growth and high double-digit FY26 growth), the stock price is valid at the moment, even with it trading above the 50-week moving average. This is no longer an undervalued stock, but it's reasonably valued and still has a moderate growth runway ahead of it. I expect that by the end of H1 2026, my price objective of about $145 will have been hit and I will be recycling the profits and initial invested capital into less cyclical and more durable growth investments. I bought Micron at an average price of $74 a share. My investment is now up by over 55%. Once I sell my position at around $145, my total return over about 12-18 months will be nearly 100%. Micron is one of my largest portfolio positions, at about 12% of my holdings. It is an exemplary selection that shows my strategy of sentiment analysis and capitalizing on cyclical dynamics for elite returns.

Cyclical Dynamics Are Registered By The Wise

Micron is in a stronger up-cycle following one of its worst down-cycles in 2023 when revenues were cut by almost half. For Q2 FY2025, revenues are about 38% higher year-over-year, with management forecasting record top-line revenue. DRAM prices, which are core to about 75% of Micron's revenue, have turned positive. NAND pricing remains weak but due to peers cutting production, has likely bottomed. The normalization of inventory, particularly in mobile and auto, affirms the argument for continued demand recovery.

Micron is trading at approximately 16.5x forward non-GAAP earnings, with FY2025 EPS of about $7 likely, meaning an increase of more than 400% from FY2024. The main discussion revolves around whether AI-led demand can extend the cycle and sustain margins. In the bull case, you could assert that AI, auto-part, and HBM leadership represent structural growth that supports an entirely new, less volatile paradigm. In the bear or base case, you could assert that cycles have always ended in oversupply, and large capacity growth could extend the cycle, but not indefinitely. I'm moderately bullish but defensive, keeping my $145 take-profit target engaged for now.

Macro Catalysts Can Impact The Cyclical Thesis

Micron's outlook is very promising; however, several risks exist. The company's $14 billion capex budget, in addition to its $200 billion expansion budget in the U.S., presents some execution risk. Any execution delays, cost overruns or a lower-than-anticipated ROI (return on investment) would negatively impact Micron's balance sheet, especially if government incentives fail to offset decreased profitability.

Geopolitical risks also loom larger as it relates to China. China has already banned certain Micron products, and any retaliation on their part could negatively impact sales, while export controls or tariffs could negatively impact Micron's supply chain or costs. I am hopeful of more peaceful U.S.-China relations moving forward, but this is dependent on state officials working diligently with state-of-the-art diplomacy and exemplary statesmanship, including emotional strategy.

Cyclicality remains a consistent risk. As previously noted, Micron is intelligently managing supply by appropriately cutting NAND supply and lowering inventory, and there is a risk of being oversupplied if we see a knee-jerk reaction from competitors or if stagnated demand (e.g., smartphones or data centers) does not meet expectations. NAND in particular could be negatively impacted.

Lastly, investor sentiment revolves greatly around management tone, and any bearish sentiment around guidance for Q4, flat gross margin outlook, or vague commentary on 2026 could indicate the peak of the cycle is closer than we’d hope. While Micron's performance in HBM and the new generation DDR5 has been very good, even a small mistake today may provide competitors the opportunity to catch up.

Keep An Eye On The Upcoming Earnings Call

The earnings call on June 25 will provide the most insight into what phase of the memory up-cycle we're in. The key earnings call questions will be: Is DRAM pricing still firm, and is NAND pricing leveling off or bottoming? If management mentions anything similar to "tight supply" or "pricing has firmed," it will support the bull case.

I’ll monitor margins closely. Q3 guidance was for 36–37% gross margins, but better than expected margin expansion depends on the level of HBM products sold and the progress of cost-per-bit improvements. Changes to margin also could be muted by underutilization of production and any inflation in input prices. Management comments about other operating expenses ("opex") will also be needed regarding future cash flow generation.

Capex guidance is another sign. If management states it will stick to the full $14B FY2025 plans, that will positively impact sentiment. I don’t expect guidance to be softened, especially as we’re gaining more clarity on the macroeconomy following a period of international trade disruption.

Forward-looking comments about the company’s end markets will help determine how strong and broad the current recovery is. AI/data center demand seems strong, but there will be a better indication that the recovery is sustainable if the mobile and PC end markets are showing signs of particular strength. Comments about inventory will also aid understanding of supply-demand equilibrium across customers and internally.

If management is still speaking confidently about corporate momentum and demand, that could support a longer holding period. At the moment, my exit strategy is shorter-term for this particular stock. If management is vague or changes tone and discusses 2026 with caution, that would add to the concern level about the cycle stabilizing or maturing. It could also rationally prompt a quicker exit for shrewd investors looking for capital security.

Conclusion: Buy

Micron is still a compelling buy, particularly in light of the firm's leadership in AI (memory) and pending expansion plans. But a defense strategy against cyclical dynamics is also called for. Micron’s current valuation embeds optimism of a stable longer-term growth phase. Yields and returns in the memory segment are improving as lower-cost, more efficient memory experiences a structural demand boost driven by AI and data centers. However, this trend also brings some risk to Micron’s capital expenditures efficacy—and the broader memory cycle and geopolitical headwinds remain primary concerns. In my view, being nimble and catching Micron’s current strong return prospects is shrewd, but it will be important to be tactical with your price objective.

data.title: HCI Vs American Coastal Insurance: HCI Continues To Be Undervalued Despite A Solid Rally
data.description:
Investment Thesis

HCI Group (NYSE:HCI) and American Coastal Insurance Corporation (NASDAQ:ACIC) are the property and casualty insurance (P&C insurance) issuers mostly concentrated in the Florida region and neighboring coastal areas. HCI is the bigger company with the product mix that caters to homeowners, landlords, and the commercial sector, while ACIC has recently sold its subsidiary to solely service the commercial sector.

Year-to-date, HCI's stock has rallied by 36%, while ACIC's has declined by 20%. Such divergence in performance tends to create a spread trade opportunity for long-short funds to bet on the spread closing. Assessing each stock's fundamental metrics, I don't think this is a good setup for such a trade, and even though both stocks still continue to be undervalued relative to the sector, investors are safe betting on the outperformer.

Historical average returns

When we look at the historical average returns, HCI has performed well relative to ACIC, SPY (SPY), and the Property and Casualty Insurance ETF (KBWP). Returning 17% in 10 years while SPY and KBWP returned 13% and ACIC returned less than 1%. Given that the P&C insurance market increased by 7.7% last year and with more growth expected as we continue to see catastrophic events worsen and the world becoming more uncertain, HCI still has more room to grow both in revenue and market cap.

ACIC's low performance can mostly be explained by its disappointing quarterly revenue growth in the past 5 years. From June 30, 2020, to the recent quarter, ACIC's revenue declined by 66%, while HCI's revenue increased by 167%.

Net loss ratio

We are all familiar with this concept: with great returns comes great exposure to risk. When we look at the revenue charts above, we can see that HCI appears to be more volatile compared to ACIC. This is because HCI is aggressively growing market share while ACIC plays a conservative card to keep the lights on. This is also explained by their net loss ratio below. ACIC puts more effort into maintaining a net loss ratio of below 30%, while HCI has a higher and more volatile chart. HCI's management has stated its commitment to reducing its loss ratio in a recent earnings call. During the first three (03) quarters of 2023, the net loss ratio averaged around 55%, and coming into 2024, the chart trended nicely to below 45%. But we started to see increasing claims, which spiked the chart to more than 60% in the second half of 2024 due to the three hurricanes (Debby, Helene, and Milton) that affected the Florida citizens and increased the number of claims from policyholders. Looking forward, I'm more optimistic that HCI will normalize the net loss ratio to below 55% as the company starts to de-risk its portfolio.

Financial Results

HCI Income Statement

In Q1 of 2025, HCI increased its gross premiums by 17% compared to Q1 of 2024 and ceded $99.63 million, retaining $200.75 million in net premiums. That was a 46% and 6% increase, respectively. Total revenue increased by 5% to $216.43 million, beating revenue estimates by $1.54 million. Operating expenses declined by 11%; this was caused by a 26% decline in incurred losses & loss adjustment expenses, showing management's commitment to decreasing the net loss ratio by ceding more of its premium. As of the last quarter Ceded premium now makes up about 33% of its gross premium. The 11% decline in OPEX increased pretax income and net income by 30%, bringing EPS to $5.35, a $0.47 and $0.79 beat of GAAP and normalized EPS estimates.

ACIC Income Statement

Looking at ACIC's Q1 income statement, total revenue increased by 8% yoy to $72.19 million, missing revenue estimates by $7.84 million. This revenue growth wasn't organic, as it was due to the company lowering its ceded premiums by 4% while growing gross premiums by only 1%. Incurred losses & loss adjustment expenses decreased by 9%, and G&A decreased by 15%, while the policy and underwriting costs increased by more than double. This resulted in a 33% increase in operating expenses and a 16% decline in operating income, which eventually led to net income declining by 10%, beating EPS estimates by only $0.02. The drastic increase in policy & underwriting costs was caused by high acquisition costs, which were due to fewer ceding commissions from reinsurers and high management fees. I understand why the company is lowering ceded premiums; as of the last quarter, the ceded premiums made up about 58% of ACIC's gross premiums. Even though this lessens the company's risk surface, it affects the bottom-line performance because more money passes through to reinsurers.

HCI Balance sheet

HCI's balance sheet shows tremendous strength and liquidity. Total investments and cash grew by 31% and 15%, respectively. Most of these investments are government bonds, and about $510 million are due for sale in a year or less, as disclosed in the 10Q. With the cash at hand, that is enough liquidity to cover the $798 million in policy claim liabilities, which increased by 38%. Reinsurance recoverables increased by 60% to $527.8 million, which implies that HCI is starting to pursue a risk-off strategy by ceding an increasing portion of its gross premiums. Looking at the liabilities side, long-term debt remained flat over the year, while unearned premiums increased by 15%, showing growth in new policy underwriting. Overall, total assets increased by 25%, liabilities by 22%, and shareholder's equity increased by 37%. On the earnings call, management further guided for another 37% increase in shareholder's equity in Q2 to close to three-quarters of $1 billion ($750 million). This guidance comes as the company expects to redeem 4.75% of its convertible senior notes, which is estimated to reduce the debt on its balance sheet by $172 million.

ACIC Balance sheet

ACIC also has a healthy balance sheet, with total investments growing by 69% to the value of $335.8 million. Cash declined by 41%, which could be explained by the surge in claim payments made in the third and the fourth quarters of 2024. Reinsurance recoverables also decreased by 21%, and ceded unearned premiums decreased by 12%. This shows that while HCI is de-risking more of its portfolio to reinsurers to lower its net loss ratio, ACIC is doing the opposite to improve its underwriting margins. The problem is unearned premium was flat over the year, showing top-line stagnation, as this implies the company is not underwriting new policies as frequently. Given this reality, margins could remain compressed even if fewer premiums get ceded. Overall, total assets increased by 8%, total liabilities increased by 3%, and shareholder's equity increased by 28%.

Quant ratings

Looking at the Seeking Alpha quant rating now vs. a year ago for HCI, we can see that the quant system made a great call rating HCI a strong buy while it was trading at $96 per share. Since then the stock has increased by 63%. Now, looking currently, not much has changed: HCI is still rated A+ for momentum, A- for profitability, growth upgraded to an A, valuation slightly downgraded to a B, and revisions drastically downgraded to a C. This has caused an overall downgrade of HCI from a strong buy to a buy. I also expect revenue and EPS to not grow significantly for HCI going forward, as it intends to reduce its net loss ratio, which might affect its underwriting margins a little bit.

The quant system rated ACIC a hold a year ago—great call again, as the stock price has remained flat since then. The system has downgraded ACIC to a sell with growth, profitability, momentum, and EPS revisions downgraded to a D-, B, D-, and D, respectively. However, the valuation has been upgraded from an A- to an A. Personally, I don't see any further decline in the stock price, and at this attractive valuation, selling might not be as smart.

Valuation

Both HCI and ACIC remain to be undervalued relative to their sector, with HCI trading at a 10% discount and ACIC trading at a 33% discount on a forward P/E ratio. Even with a 36% stock performance YTD, there's still room for HCI to continue outperforming, while ACIC signals to be a classical value trap that is nearing the bottom. Given the solid fundamentals HCI has, I don't see any slowdown in stock performance and believe the spread between HCI and ACIC will continue to widen.

Risks

As we enter the second half of 2025, hurricanes and other catastrophic events are likely to happen, which will obviously be a short-term headwind for both stocks, causing more claims and compressing margin returns. HCI continues to have a higher net loss ratio than ACIC, meaning it is much more vulnerable to these catastrophes. It should be stated, though, that the company managed to grow shareholder equity by $150 million last year despite having to deal with three (03) hurricanes. However, past performance is not indicative of future performance. Investors should also take note of the news that HCI was listed as one of the companies that are going to leave the Russell microcap index, even though I do not consider this to be a massive risk, but the index outflows could potentially result in high volatility in HCI's stock.

Conclusion

In closing, I find HCI to be well positioned for an upside revaluation as the market continues to appreciate the quality metrics of this company, i.e., double-digit revenue growth, decreasing operating expenses, and a highly liquid, de-levered balance sheet. Meanwhile, ACIC has stagnant top-line growth, compressed profit margins, and a historically bad performance. Not everything is bearish on ACIC; the company has a healthy balance sheet, and it has a less risky portfolio. With that said, I rate HCI a buy and ACIC a hold.

Kindly note that going forward I will start to do more comparative analyses. This came from feedback I received from my prior articles; one was (GAMB) vs. (RSI) and the other was (AL) vs. (AER). These articles got positive feedback from my followers and SA editors.

Fortunately, these articles happened to be one of my favorite write-ups and the type of content I find to be lacking in most research platforms. So going forward, expect more comparative analysis from me; however, I will continue to do single stock write-ups occasionally. Your feedback is highly appreciated.

data.title: Shopify Can Easily Cross $150B+ Market Cap In H2 2025
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Shopify (SHOP) in March, I rated the stock a “buy” with a price target of $125. Although the stock dropped soon afterwards given the market volatility from post-Liberation Day announcements, it has been able to climb back up, with the stock now down just 1.5% since the time of my previous publication.

Shopify reported their Q1 FY25 earnings in May, where revenue growth slowed sequentially to 27% YoY as expected, with an operating margin of 9%, which also shrank on a quarter-over-quarter basis.

While the slowdown was already priced in, their total GMV (Gross Merchandise Volume) grew 23%, marking the seventh consecutive quarter of twenty percent-plus growth rates, as the company continued to see strength in its International, Offline and Enterprise/B2B markets. Moreover, the penetration of Shopify Payments as a percentage of GMV continued to deepen, especially as the company launched it in 16 new countries during the quarter.

Although the current macroeconomic environment remains challenging, the management remained confident in their ability to navigate the landscape given the quality and diversity of their merchant and user base, as well as the agility of their “merchant-first” product innovation.

Looking forward, I continue to remain bullish on Shopify, as I believe that there is plenty of whitespace that the company can penetrate, especially as it expands across the types of merchants across geographies by innovating on their product portfolio, thus solidifying their value proposition as the “unified operating system for commerce”.

As a result, I will reiterate my price target of $124, which implies an 18% upside or a market cap that should reach close to $160B by the end of the year.

Deepening Penetration Of Shopify Payments And Merchant-First Product Innovation

Shopify reported their Q1 FY25 earnings last month, where revenue grew 27% YoY to $2.36B, while GMV grew close to 23% YoY, marking the seventh consecutive quarter of twenty percent-plus growth rates, driven by growth in both same-store sales and new merchant acquisitions.

Out of the $2.36B in revenue, Merchant Solutions revenue grew 29% to $1.74B, with the penetration of Shopify Payments reaching 65% of GMV, up from 60% last year, while Subscription Solutions revenue increased 21% YoY to $620M as a result of new merchant additions, variable platform fees, and a growing adoption of Shopify Plus subscription plan.

With a value proposition that is centered on building a unified operating system for commerce, Shopify has always taken a “merchant-first” focus when it comes to product innovation.

During the quarter, the company continued to expand the breadth of their product portfolio by enhancing their managed markets products for cross-border trade, thus enabling their US merchants to achieve compliance with ease. At the same time, Shopify also introduced a feature on their Shop App that allows buyers to filter products by country, thus promoting local businesses.

Plus, in order to help merchants lower their duty rates when it comes to sourcing products from different countries in the current macroeconomic climate, Shopify launched their AI-driven tariff tool called tariffguide.ai while simultaneously simplifying international shipping by expanding their network of third-party logistics providers through the Shopify Fulfillment Network App.

This has helped the company drive deeper adoption of its platform, with continuing strength across all their revenue cohorts, as can be seen below. In fact, according to management commentary, 38 out of 39 of their quarterly merchant cohorts have outperformed the overall e-commerce market, indicating the platform’s ability to drive growth and resilience.

3 Growth Levers To Capture TAM: International, Offline And Enterprise

Despite Shopify growing robustly ever since it IPO’ed in 2015, their core market still has plenty of runway, as they continue to add more products, expand across more geographies, and serve merchants of varying sizes to capture market share in a growing TAM (Total Addressable Market).

In fact, per the slide below, Shopify has only penetrated just 2% of opportunities in geographies they currently serve, leaving plenty of whitespace to expand into in the coming years.

Particularly, when it comes to its growth levers, I want to briefly discuss three of its strategic priorities.

Let’s start with Shopify’s International markets, which saw GMV grow 31% YoY, compared to 33% in the previous quarter, driven by growth across both small merchants and enterprises in key European markets that include the UK, Netherlands, and Germany.

During the quarter, the company launched Shopify Payments in 16 new markets, bringing the total to 39 countries that are now supported by Shopify Payments, thus offering merchants a streamlined payment solution with lower fees and enhanced security, as well as access to features like Shop Pay that provide greater convenience for buyers. Plus, they also launched multicurrency payouts in 20 countries across Europe, thus helping buyers minimize the impact of fluctuating exchange rates.

Meanwhile, Shopify’s Offline GMV also grew 23% YoY as a result of strength in both their mid-market and multi-location merchants.

Finally, the company continues to make progress with moving upmarket. During the quarter, Shopify reported that its B2B GMV delivered triple-digit growth of 109% YoY. One of the products that the management credited for its success in moving upmarket is Shop Pay, where it acts as a strategic entry point for enterprise brands to enter the Shopify ecosystem, followed by a gradual adoption across other products.

Plus, in the current macroeconomic environment, where legacy platforms are struggling to adapt rapidly, Shopify is able to help merchants quickly navigate the evolving landscape, particularly around tariffs, with ease and efficiency, thus enabling them to onboard larger companies across geographies.

Gross Margins Shrank YoY, But Operating Expenses As A Percentage Of Revenue Trended Lower

One of the things that the management reiterated numerous times on the earnings call was their commitment to their operating discipline, which they believe is key to providing the flexibility they need to ensure long-term growth.

During the quarter, Shopify saw its gross margins expand on a sequential basis by 140 basis points to 49.5%. However, on a year-over-year basis, gross margins declined close to 190 basis points as a result of higher cloud and infrastructure hosting costs needed to support higher volumes and geographic expansion.

In the meantime, the company generated $203M in operating income, which grew over 136% YoY with a margin expansion of 400 basis points to 9%, with operating expenses that continue to trend lower as a percentage of total revenue (as can be seen below), as the management remains committed to growing operating leverage by building a lean, flexible, and highly efficient team.

Tariff Risks Remain, Although Management Is Confident It Can Navigate Through With Ease

Although Shopify is expected to grow in the twenties range over the coming years as per consensus estimates, the current macroeconomic uncertainty around tariffs and its impact on the consumer and business demand landscape can induce volatility in the company.

So far, the management has reiterated that they haven’t seen any “meaningful” impact on GMV, given the quality and diversity of their merchant base and buyers, which the management believes provides “unique stability” to their business and allows them to navigate changing macroeconomic conditions with ease.

At the same time, the management also noted that they haven’t seen broad-based price increases across goods sold by merchants on their platform amid tariff pressures. Even if merchants were to raise prices in the future, the management believes that they are more insulated from demand shocks as their buyer base skews towards higher-income consumers, who tend to be less price sensitive.

So far, the company’s track record on delivering earnings surprises has been quite uneven, while it has consistently beat revenue guidance. Looking forward, the tariff environment is one to watch out for and how it impacts GMV growth across all its markets. The way I see it, as long as GMV growth stays in the 20%+ range, with growing penetration of Shopify Payments, investor sentiment should remain anchored.

Revisiting My Valuation

As Shopify continues to expand the types of merchants they serve across geographies by helping them grow their businesses with the help of innovative solutions that deepen their usage across the platform, I believe that the company has tremendous runway left in its $849B TAM.

From a revenue perspective, Shopify is expected to grow in the twenties range over the coming years, which, I believe, is quite achievable, as it doubles down on its growth levers of International, Offline and Enterprise/B2B markets with growing penetration of Shopify Payments and Subscription Solutions. This will translate to an estimated revenue of close to $16B by FY27.

When it comes to profitability, assuming that Shopify can reach gross margins of 52% (up from 50% in FY24) by FY27, along with streamlining its operating expenses to 35% of total revenue during this period of time, we arrive at a total operating income of $2.7B, with a margin of 17%, up from 12% in FY24.

This means that the company is expected to grow its operating income at a compounded annual growth rate of 36% over the coming three years, as opposed to the average company in the S&P 500 that grows its earnings at a pace of 8%. Therefore, assigning it a price-to-earnings multiple of 77, which is roughly 4.5 times that of the average S&P 500 multiple, we arrive at a price target of $124, which represents an upside of 18% from its current levels, or a market cap of close to $160B.

My Final Verdict And Conclusions

Although the management remains confident in their ability to navigate the current macroeconomic landscape around tariffs, we need to monitor the trend of GMV in the short term to assess the shift in the demand landscape.

However, over the mid- to long-term, I believe the stock is headed significantly higher, with the market cap of Shopify likely exceeding $160B by the end of the year as it continues to grab market share by expanding across the types of merchants they serve across geographies and building targeted solutions that further deepen adoption of the platform.

data.title: Discount Dividends: 2 Irresistible Bargains Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
Introduction

I have good news.

The market is close to its all-time high again, erasing a decline of roughly 20%, which made it one of five corrections over the past ten years.

While it needs to be seen just how bad news the Iran war headlines are for the market, there is no denying that the world's biggest stock index is holding up impressively well.

This has a number of reasons, including the assurance that we are, in fact, not headed for a global trade war that could blow up supply chains. While we are still in a headline-driven market with tariff risks, the worst-case scenario has not happened, causing investors to slowly but steadily bet on a "return to normal."

It also helps that the Federal Reserve is increasingly expected to lower rates, as Fed funds futures (which always need to be taken with a grain of salt) indicate four rate cuts over the next 12 months, potentially lowering rates to the range of 3.25% to 3.50%, all fueled by the absence of any upside momentum in inflation numbers, even after tariffs have been implemented.

While all of this is good news, we are not out of the woods yet, as Bloomberg's John Authers wrote in an opinion piece titled "Waiting for the Fed All Summer Long" earlier this week.

He brought up one of the most important points I can think of, which is the hidden risk of higher-for-longer inflation.

One problem is that the underlying numbers simply don't look that good yet. It's one of the reasons why I believe in a "higher for longer" inflation environment, as the underlying inflation beast hasn't been defeated yet.

Over the last three months, flexible prices dipped, possibly due to weak demand, while sticky price inflation declined very slowly. The Cleveland Fed’s measure of the trimmed mean, which excludes outliers and averages the rest, ticked upward for the first time this year, and remains above 3% — another sign that underlying inflation pressures remain. - Bloomberg

These problems include the "supercore" inflation number, which is services excluding shelter. This index is heavily watched by the Federal Reserve, as inflation in services has been a major problem for the economy since the pandemic. That's because inflation in goods worked its way through the economy, hitting the one area that hurts the most, which is services.

Also, to make matters worse, goods inflation is making a comeback.

These developments make sense, as manufacturing companies noted a steep increase in delivery times and prices paid, two leading indicators for inflation, as it usually takes a few months for these prices to work their way through the supply chain.

Also, I am worried about oil.

By that, I mean the impact of oil on inflation. Due to OPEC aggressively boosting output and economic growth fears, oil has made it to the low-$60s. Without that benefit to inflation, these numbers would look very different.

That's one of the reasons why I expect any meaningful increase in oil to further put a strong foundation below inflation and make it harder for the Fed to cut rates.

The chart below shows what the price of oil (black line) compared to the all-item year-over-year inflation rate in the United States (purple line) looks like:

That's why I'm going back to John Authers again, as he brought up comments from ABN Amro Investment Solutions, which summarize perfectly the challenges that some may be missing:

  • Slow disinflation is not enough to prompt the Fed to change its stance. Only a major downside surprise on jobs data, which seems unlikely, may urge it to cut rates.
  • If there is a Liberation Day Part 2 where Trump announces widespread tariffs, including a 50% tariff on Europe, the risk of a Trumpcession may resurface.

To make matters worse, this challenging market environment comes with a lofty valuation. Because of the market's rebound, the valuation has become anything but cheap.

As much fun as I had buying bargains in April, the fact that the S&P 500's earnings yield premium has dropped to a near two-decade low makes it much harder to find great stocks at attractive prices.

The data above is extremely important, as the equity risk premium compares the S&P 500's earnings yield to the yield investors get for buying the 10-year bond.

Essentially, the market's earnings yield is the inverse P/E ratio. In order to explain why this matters, imagine that this is the dividend yield of the market if all earnings were to be paid through dividends. The higher, the more value investors are buying.

When comparing the number to the yield on risk-free bonds, we get the relative attractiveness of stocks.

Objectively and generally speaking, right now, stocks are not that attractive, as the earnings yield is less than the yield on 10-year bonds.

That's partially why I expect the total return of the S&P 500 to be subdued over the next 5–10 years, at least compared to the past ten years.

However, I am not shying away from stocks. On the contrary. As most readers will know, I dislike bonds because I prefer to use my hard-earned cash to buy stakes in fantastic companies instead of lending it to governments.

That's why I am spending so much time on finding stocks that come with attractive valuations, fantastic business models, dividend growth/income, and a high likelihood to generate substantial alpha when it matters most.

So, in light of everything we discussed, which includes sticky inflation, growth challenges, and valuation headwinds, I decided to focus on two stocks that deliver exactly what I am looking for.

However, to justify this very bullish title, I made sure to really emphasize the valuation part, as I expect both stocks to be realistically undervalued by 50-100% over the next 3–5 years.

Permian Resources Corp. (PR) - One Of The Best Risk-Reward Ratios On The Market

I was 100% sure I was going to include an energy stock in this article.

Historically speaking, energy stocks are the best place to be in times of elevated inflation, as they have the highest percentage of positive total returns and the highest outperformance when inflation averages 3% or more on a longer-term basis.

This means energy stocks are perfect for my thesis.

It also means I could mention at least a dozen great energy picks.

However, for this specific article, I decided to go with Permian Resources, a company I started covering in 2024, when I called it "The Cheapest Oil Stock You've Never Heard Of."

Permian Resources is NOT for the faint of heart, as its market cap of $10.4 billion makes it much smaller than the reliable dividend energy companies like Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX) you may be more familiar with. It's also much more volatile. So, please keep that in mind when doing your own due diligence.

With that said, I also believe that PR is much more promising than most of its bigger peers. For a wide range of reasons, actually.

One reason is its focus on the Permian Basin, America's biggest oil basin. It's also the only major basin capable of consistent output growth, thanks to large untapped resources in both the Midland and Delaware sub-basins.

Headquartered in Midland, Texas, Permian Resources' assets cover roughly 450,000 net acres and 88,000 net royalty acres, which are mainly located in Eddy and Lea Counties, New Mexico, and Reeves and Ward Counties, Texas. These are some of my favorite places to be and major areas of operations of LandBridge (LB) and Texas Pacific Land (TPL), my two biggest investments.

The company, which went through the 2015/2016 energy crash as Colgate Energy, has a strategy built on three pillars, which are cost leadership, a strong balance sheet, and opportunistic investments during market downturns.

While the company is highly dependent on energy prices, which limits its ability to generate value beyond traditional energy operations, it has found an edge thanks to characteristics like a high oil share of total revenues. As of 1Q25, oil accounted for 47% of its revenues, with high-margin liquids boosting that number to 70%.

It also had a net leverage ratio of just 0.8x, one of the best balance sheets in the entire industry, which also comes with a robust hedging position, as PR has hedged a quarter of its production with a floor of $73 WTI.

While I prefer unhedged companies during oil bull markets, it's a great strategy that actually beats even peers like Diamondback (FANG) when it comes to downside protection. I'm impressed by that.

Moreover, it has done tremendously well when it comes to operating efficiencies, growing its oil production by 2% since 4Q24 with 4% lower cash costs per barrel and a 3% decline in drilling and completion costs.

On top of that, it has more than 15 years of high-quality inventory, which is consistently expanding through bolt-on acquisitions and new discoveries.

The best thing, however, is that Permian Resources is really good at distributing cash to shareholders. Instead of simply buying back stock and paying a dividend, the company is great at timing.

Currently, it has a buyback authorization of $1 billion (nearly 10% of its market cap). In April, it bought back $43 million of stock. While that's not a lot, it did so at the bottom of its stock price, with goals to use future dips to deploy more capital. This makes future corrections great opportunities to improve the risk/reward of the company.

It also declared a quarterly base cash dividend of $0.15 per share, which translates to $0.60 per year. At current prices, that's a 4.0% yield that's fully breakeven at $40 WTI, a number that many of its peers cannot match.

That's why I expect aggressive, double-digit, annual dividend growth if I'm right and oil starts a long-term bull market in the quarters ahead.

It also remains one of the cheapest stocks.

Compared to its peers, it has one of the lowest enterprise value multiples. Although the stock price has risen by roughly 17% since the overview below was made, it shows the company trades at a substantial discount to larger peers.

While it deserves to trade at a small discount due to its smaller size and the fact that investors still feel that the company needs to prove itself, its balance sheet and operational excellence warrant a much smaller gap to peers like Diamondback.

In fact, PR trades at just 3x operating cash flow, a number I consider to be ridiculously cheap, as it's way below its long-term average of 5.2x, and even further below the 7-9x average I consider to be appropriate for high-quality Permian drillers.

As a result, I expect PR to double over the next 12–24 months. That's my base case. A stronger oil price uptrend could unlock a lot more value.

Canadian National Railway (CNI) - A Canadian Gem That's Perfect For What's Next

I like low-risk contrarian investments. Investments that usually perform well but are out of favor due to issues like cyclical growth headwinds.

That's why I have taken a shine to cyclical stocks this year. While I fully acknowledge the economic headwinds facing the market in almost every single article I wrote, it's one of my favorite places to put money in a market that has become quite expensive again.

In May, for example, I wrote an article titled "Buried Under Fear: One Of The Best Opportunities I've Seen In Years." One of the companies I should have included in that article is the Canadian National Railway, a company whose roots go back to 1919, when it was created out of seven bankrupt railroads that fell into the hands of the Canadian government.

Back then, railroading was hard, as competition was fierce. There were simply too many railroads on the market. There was not a lot of money to be made unless you had the best assets and a unique game plan.

Now, that's different, as North America is home to just six major Class I railroads, with one of these being Buffett's private BNSF, which can only be owned through Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A).

In fact, all Class I railroads have wide-moat business models thanks to very limited competition, dominant positions in the areas they serve, strong pricing power, efficiency benefits compared to on-the-road trucking, and other things like healthy balance sheets.

Canadian National is one of them, as it operates a huge rail network of roughly 20,000 route miles across Canada and the United States, which connects three coasts: the Atlantic, the Pacific, and the Gulf of Mexico.

Moreover, the company transports a wide and diverse range of goods, including bulk commodities (grain, coal, potash), intermodal, forest products, and energy products like petroleum and chemicals. This makes it "mission-critical" in almost every supply chain you can think of.

In general, I like that just roughly a fifth of its revenue comes from intermodal, with most revenues being generated by higher-margin bulk and merchandise goods. Intermodal is more dependent on consumer sentiment and has stronger competition from trucks carrying containers from ports to customers. It's not a bad business, but I simply prefer bulk and merch.

This has allowed the company's New York-listed shares to return 3,520% since January 1, 2000, beating the 540% return of the market by a wide margin.

Also, note that industrial stocks have beaten the market since then, which, I believe, is due to the wide-moat business models of many industrial stocks. While most don't have the high-tech advantages of some of today's high-flying companies, they tend to come with fewer disruption risks and more consistent growth. That's one of the reasons why I like industrial stocks too much.

Anyway, like all of its peers, since 2021, CNI has been a disappointment. That's due to a peak in cyclical growth expectations, worsened by trade war fears, labor disruption, and smaller factors.

However, the risk/reward is getting good!

On top of achieving 8% EPS growth in 1Q25, the company has seen velocity metrics reaching levels not seen in May for several years, as the Canadian is doing what it does best, which is boosting its operating efficiencies.

It also reiterated its full-year guidance last month, expecting 10% to 15% EPS growth despite tough volume comparisons in the second quarter. Even better, the company expects a stronger performance in the second half of the year, driven by easier comparisons, company-specific growth initiatives, and a potential economic uptick, which is why I wrote my "Buried In Fear" article.

These company-specific investments include customer-driven volume growth from fracking sand supply for LNG Canada, liquid exports to Japan, and new crush facilities in Saskatchewan and Ohio.

On a long-term basis, the company targets high-single-digit adjusted EPS growth, growing faster than the economy, with pricing ahead of rail inflation.

This also means that if we were to get a cyclical growth upswing, the company could see substantially higher growth rates.

Analysts agree with that, as they expect 16%, 11%, and 10% EPS growth in 2025, 2026, and 2027, respectively, potentially marking the first sustainable upswing since the pandemic.

As CNI trades at just 19.7x earnings, below its 10-year average of 21.3x, there's a path to a fair price target of $150 in New York, roughly 50% above its current price.

That's why I absolutely love the risk/reward.

If I did not already own Union Pacific (UNP) and Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP), CNI would be a holding of mine as well, as there are not a lot of people who love railroads more than me.

The cherry on top of its dividend.

Currently yielding 2.5%, the dividend has been raised every single year since 1995, with a 15% CAGR since 1996. This year, the hike was 5%, which I expect to be boosted in the years ahead. It has a sub-50% payout ratio and an A-range credit rating, providing additional safety.

All things considered, just like PR, CNI is a Strong Buy in my book.

Takeaway

The market's resilience is impressive. However, we are not out of the woods yet.

Inflation remains sticky, oil is a wildcard, and valuations have become stretched again. However, I'm not sitting on the sidelines. As always, I'm hunting for stocks that offer exceptional value in this environment.

Permian Resources is one of my high-conviction energy plays, as it's a lean, efficient operator with a rock-solid balance sheet, aggressive buybacks, and a dividend that could soar if oil rallies.

Meanwhile, Canadian National Railway is a cyclical gem trading at a steep discount, with earnings poised to accelerate and a dividend that's grown nonstop since my birth year of 1995.

All things considered, both stocks have realistic 50-100% upside potential in the next few years. Depending on one's growth profile, they could make great additions to a wide variety of dividend portfolios.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk is prolonged cyclical weakness, which could pressure oil demand, transportation volumes, and general market sentiment. Specific risks are more OPEC output hikes, tariffs on Canada that could disrupt cross-border flows, and operational struggles in CNI's network.

data.title: Ategrity Specialty Holdings: A Strong Specialty Insurance Offering
data.description:
Shares of Ategrity Specialty Holdings (NYSE:ASIC) have seen a rather strong public debut. Shares of the specialty insurance firm have come strong out of the gate, and have risen as investors like the focused operations, earnings and reasonable valuations at the offer price.

While these qualities are demonstrated in the growth of the business, I am not a big investor in insurance operations, given the inherent risks involved, although I recognize that the industry at large has a solid track record in creating value for investors.

A Specialty Insurance Firm

Ategrity Specialty describes itself as a growing specialty insurance business which focuses on so-called "excess and surplus" products, which it offers to small and medium-sized businesses. The specific segment focuses on insurance of items which standard carriers are not covering.

Special verticals on which the firm focuses include retail, real estate, hospitality and construction. The company focuses mostly on short-term and medium-term policies, with premiums for nearly two-thirds tied towards casualty, and the remainder from property insurance.

The company has developed a proprietary underwriting platform which combines sophisticated data analytics with automated and streamlined processes to serve clients and shareholders. To serve end clients, the company uses over 500 distribution partners as customers have greater need for such tech-enabled solutions.

The company generates revenues in 48 states, yet the combined revenues generated from California, Florida and Texas totals about 50% of revenues. With some of these states vulnerable to specific (natural) disasters, that is suboptimal.

Valuation and IPO Thoughts

Ategrity Specialty aimed to sell 6.7 million shares in a preliminary price range between $14 and $16 per share, with final pricing set at $17 per share. With all shares sold by the business itself, gross proceeds from the offering came in at $114 million, that is, of course, excluding the over-allotment option.

With just over 47 million shares outstanding, the equity of the business is valued at $799 million at the offer price. The balance sheet is a bit hard to read, given the nature of the business and its operations.

The same complexity is observed in the profit and loss statement. The company reported net premiums earned of $231 million in 2023, with investment income driving total revenues to $241 million. The company reported just $12 million in operating income, with the loss ratio reported around two-thirds, and the expense ratio topping 30% of sales.

Net premiums earned rose to over $290 million in 2024, with total revenues up to $343 million and change, driven by much higher investment income and alike. This resulted in operating expenses having risen to a $66 million number, with net earnings reported at $47 million, which incidentally was equal to a dollar per share.

Net premium earned for the first quarter of 2025 rose by more than ten million to $78 million, as the increase in total revenues was a bit less due to a lower net sum of investment income and gains on investments. Amidst higher costs, operating earnings were down by an increment, with GAAP earnings reported at $8 million and change.

While revenues are growing, I conservatively look for earnings to come in flat this year, suggesting that earnings come in around a dollar per share, as the company might see some benefits from the gross proceeds of the offering. This could boost after-tax income by a few million and thereby a couple of pennies per share as well.

As shares have risen to $23 in the days following the offering, the market value of the firm tops a billion, and with earnings power seen around a dollar per share, or just above that, multiples are rather demanding here.

Concluding Thoughts

I am not too keen on investing in insurance businesses, despite the float available to invest. As an industry outsider, it is hard to judge the risk strategic of such firms and investments, as I fear adverse outcomes for insurers.

Risk factors have much to do with underwriting and losses in adverse events, fierce competition, an inability to obtain reinsurance, counterparty credit risk, reliance on distribution partners, extreme weather, concentrated regions in which policies are underwritten, and the fact that the company is a controlled company, with shareholder ZFSG holding a controlling stake in the business.

All this, but mostly plain valuations, make it very easy to avoid the shares after the public offering. This follows solid gains in the first days of trading, pushing up multiples to a premium over the market at large here. This makes me very cautious here, as I am taking a wait-and-see-approach to learn more about the business and its performance in the coming quarters, but in no rush to get involved here.

data.title: Clarus: Promotionality Hitting Margins
data.description:
Clarus (CLAR) did not manage to drive growth yet as it continues to simplify its business aiming to reach a steady state profitability. Being in transition, they are hit by a few non-recurring mix effects and also some other transitory costs. They have withdrawn guidance on uncertainty around recent May pricing actions and also questions around margin effects from tariffs which have required considerable price increases on their Black Diamond flagship branded products. While the transition will likely help underlying economics, we don't really see the business becoming a profitable grower very soon, particularly as they attempt price increases to offset tariffs. As of now, considering also the possible dangers on the demand side, multiples are looking quite in line with peers as well, though it's difficult to say with the business operating around breakeven, and P/S multiples being less useful as we wonder about terminal profitability. We wait and see.

Latest Quarter

As opposed to the FY 2024, the latest quarter saw a weaker result, particularly in international adventure. Revenue declines were occurring here due to quite a lot of promotionality on slow-moving inventory, pushed through an off-price retailer. This also had a negative mix effect. This shouldn't be recurring, but it's part of the voiding of inventory that they are likely to deprioritise going forward. Tickets weren't that high on account of this, and there were also general delays to major retailers in key markets. It appears that these OEM delays should be mostly a negative timing effect, and mostly explain the declines in Q1, but will also accompany difficulties in returning demand from the existing distributors to normalised levels.

Outdoor is also experiencing negative mix effects. The share of discontinued products in the mix is up by around 50%, as they exit businesses like bindings and also leave snow safety. It is being investigated by the DoJ over the avalanche safety, being recently served subpoenas. This is generating legal fees that explains more than 20% of the net segment operating loss this quarter. The business is getting simplified at least, but the pushing of discontinued merchandise has resulted in significant promotionality in this segment as well, and the discontinuing of the businesses also means significant sales impacts, with the ski category down 38% in North America wholesale. Also, the business is getting hit by some timing effects where a better cadence was established that frontloaded some of the sales to distributors into the Q4 at the expense of the current Q1, to our understanding. But things are getting a little more efficient. D2C unit economics have started to improve in outdoor NA sales.

Bottom Line

There are some bright spots. While sales are still down, bookings are looking better, with a good order book for the fall/winter season with apparel bookings at least up 30-50% depending on geography compared to last year. Black Diamond is now also set up with ecommerce. Headcount is 25% lower, sales are down by less, so there has been at least a per-head productivity improvement. Gross margins are also especially bad this quarter due to the aggressive promotionality to clear unwanted inventory.

On the other hand, the steel and aluminium tariffs have required price increases, just instituted this May, and while management reports things look alright as of the earnings call, there is a risk that markets don't absorb this pricing action favourably. Then there are the general macro questions around the impact of tariffs. At least on the cost side the exposure could be worse. It seems that 25% of the total merchandise cost for the company is from China. It is focused on adventure which is mostly China exposed but also more international facing. Therefore, the exposure shouldn't be too bad to China at least where higher tariffs are likely to stick. Brands like Black Diamond have 25% overall exposure on the supply side to China. But they are accelerating plans to move away. Still, there are the other universal and steel and aluminium tariffs that will have an important effect, and they are trying to offset these tariffs with price increases that are quite considerable.

Then there is also this emerging legal matter, that we are not qualified to comment on in detail other than to say it adds to the uncertainty.

While outdoor EBIT margins have improved on simplification despite negative mix effects, it may struggle to see enough improvement to help cover the corporate costs. Corporate costs on an adjusted basis were 2.3 million USD. They are still a ways away from solid profitability. We are going to wait and see at this point. We want to know how the sales materialise towards the end of the year in the current consumer environment, particularly as they attempt to pass higher steel and aluminum, or Chinese tariff costs, onto consumers. We are not confident that pricing action will be well met by the current consumer environment in general, as a macro question in the wake of tariffs. We also want to see how the attenuation of these transitory negative mix effects resolve into results, and also how the new delivery cadence looks over the full year.

data.title: AbbVie's Post-Humira Strategy Is Paying Off For Investors
data.description:
Investing Thesis

As an income-oriented investor, I am always on the lookout for high-yielding stocks with strong track records of dividend growth and promising trajectories for continued growth moving forward. When I first began investing, I made a few novice mistakes, the most notable of which was chasing high yields without really considering the company's underlying strengths or weaknesses. Not surprisingly, I bought into a few yield traps and watched as dwindling share prices and dividend cuts dinged my portfolio. Over time, my investing strategies evolved, and I outgrew my simplistic "high-yield at any cost" approach. One side effect of these growing pains, though, has been a persistent skepticism aimed at high-yielding equities, which has occasionally prevented me from initiating a position in a promising stock. One company I shied away from was AbbVie, Inc. (ABBV), whose yield ranged from 4%-7% between 2019 and 2022, the period during which I initially started eyeing the stock. Although I was aware of the company's status as a Dividend Aristocrat, that deep-seated skepticism about high yields and stagnant share prices kept me on the sidelines until the share price finally broke out of its range, at which time I promptly decided to wait for the price to come back down:

Needless to say, I never got off the sidelines because the share price stayed up while the yield dropped below its formerly high levels.

In this article, I will evaluate AbbVie as a potential investment for dividend growth investors and determine whether now would be a good time to finally initiate a position in the company.

Company Overview

Spun off from Abbott Laboratories (ABT) in 2013 and headquartered in North Chicago, Illinois, AbbVie is one of the largest biopharmaceutical companies operating in the world today. The spin-off was designed to separate the parent company's research-oriented pharmaceutical development (now branded as AbbVie) from operations aimed at marketing medical devices, generic medicines, and nutritional products. While AbbVie is largely known for its highly successful Humira, it has recently found similar levels of success with Rinvoq and Skyrizi.

A Look at AbbVie's Finances

In the company's most recent quarterly report, AbbVie raised its full-year earnings per share guidance for 2025 from a range of $11.99-$12.19 per share to a range of $12.09-$12.29, reflecting management's sense that the company has been and will continue to outperform expectations as a result of strong and growing demand for Rinvoq and Skyrizi:

In reporting a net revenue of $13.343 billion, AbbVie marked a year-over-year increase of 8.4%. The company's adjusted earnings per share of $2.46 marked a 6.5% increase compared with the same quarter in 2024, suggesting top-line growth that supports profitability and the generation of free cash flow:

Importantly, these positive numbers come in the wake of AbbVie's loss of Humira's exclusivity in the United States. This trend stems from the company's strong immunology segment, powered by Rinvoq and Skyrizi. Thanks to the pair of popular treatments, AbbVie's immunology sales hit $6.264 billion, which represents an impressive 16.6% jump year-over-year. Of the two, Skyrizi generated more profit, raking in $3.425 billion, while Rinvoq contributed an additional $1.718 billion.

The Dividend

When AbbVie raised its quarterly dividend from $1.55 per share to $1.64 per share in February, the company extended its streak of annual dividend increases to a whopping 52 consecutive years, if we include the company's history as part of Abbott Laboratories. In the dozen years since AbbVie spun off from its parent in 2013 alone, the company has increased its quarterly dividend by 310% while maintaining a generous yield in the neighborhood of 3%-4%:

This recent history emphasizes AbbVie's commitment to rewarding shareholders with a growing yield that is substantially higher than that of the healthcare sector. If we use the iShares U.S. Healthcare ETF (IYH) as a proxy for the sector, we see that AbbVie's 3.34% is more than double that of its sector's 1.27%:

Importantly, AbbVie's generous dividend growth has not come at the expense of total shareholder return. Rather, AbbVie investors have enjoyed substantially better rates of return than they would have had they invested in the healthcare sector more broadly:

Not surprisingly, AbbVie has outperformed such industry peers as Amgen (AMGN), Gilead Sciences (GILD), Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), CSL, Ltd. (OTCQX:CSLLY), and Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, Inc (REGN) over the past five years:

While the compound annual growth rate of AbbVie's dividend has slowed somewhat over the past few years, dropping from a 10-year CAGR of 13.2%, to a 5-year CAGR of 7.2% and a 3 years CAGR of 5.6%, the company does appear to have settled on a medium-term range of 5%-7% increases annually. While the growth rate may not be as high as it once was, AbbVie's already generous yield provides investors with both current income and moderate future dividend growth prospects.

AbbVie's current payout ratio of 62.12% is comfortably covered by the company's free cash flow (at 75% of FCF) and appears to be about as solid as one could hope for in an industry such as healthcare.

What's in the Pipeline?

Of course, AbbVie's ability to continue paying and raising dividends ultimately comes down to the company's capacity for transitioning from legacy drugs such as its flagship Humira to newer offerings such as Skyrizi and Rinvoq. In the two years since Humira's U.S. patent exclusivity ended in 2023, ten biosimilar products have launched, reducing Humira's sales by about 40%. Given that Humira had been the world's top-selling drug prior to its loss of exclusivity, AbbVie has had to replace a major source of revenue with income produced by other drugs.

Fortunately, for AbbVie investors, both Skyrizi and Rinvoq have helped the company cope with Humira's loss of exclusivity. In the company's first quarter financial results for 2025, AbbVie reported that Humira's sales plummeted 50% to $1.12 billion. At the same time, the company's non-Humira catalog grew by more than 21% on a year-over-year basis, thanks in large part to impressive sales of the aforementioned Skyrizi and Rinvoq. One significant driver in the two drugs' growth has been their adoption as treatments for atopic dermatitis in addition to their core targets of Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis. Furthermore, the company expects five additional indications for Rinvoq over the next several years, which should continue to drive growth. With the success of these two immunology drugs, AbbVie now anticipates $24.7 billion in combined sales in 2025, raising its previously issued guidance by close to $900 million. In fact, management expects the combined sales of Rinvoq and Skyrizi to reach $31 million by 2027, overtaking Humira's pre-LOE sales.

While Rinvoq and Skyrizi are poised to drive AbbVie's growth for the time being, the company also has a number of promising candidates in its pipeline, ranging from oncological treatments to drugs targeted towards autoimmune disorders, inflammation, and metabolic conditions and several treatments aimed at neurological disorders:

  • Oncology: AbbVie is currently in early clinical testing for ABBV-969, a novel antibody-drug conjugate aimed at metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. Another treatment, ABBV-514, is currently in trials for non-small cell lung cancer and head & neck squamous cell carcinoma. ABBV-383 and ABBV-400, which target multiple myeloma and solid tumors such as those found in the colon or lung, are now progressing through Phase 1/2 towards Phase 3. Lastly, Teliso-V, which AbbVie acquired from its purchase of ImmunoGen last year, targets c‑Met–positive non‑small cell lung cancer and has shown encouraging results in tests.
  • Autoimmune conditions, inflammation, and obesity: In addition to a few new indications for Skyrizi and Rinvoq (hidradenitis suppurativa, TL1A combo trials) that are currently in development, AbbVie is working to bring NX-13, a mid-stage ulcerative colitis drug acquired from Landos Biopharmaceutical, to market to join the company's two bellwethers in the irritable bowel disease market. Elsewhere, AbbVie entered into the obesity treatment market by paying the Danish firm Gubra $350 million for the rights to GUBamy, a long-acting amylin analog that is currently in Phase 1 trials.
  • Neurological Treatments: AbbVie's Tavapadon is currently in Phase 3 trials and shows promise as a treatment for Parkinson's Disease, having reached its primary endpoint. Elsewhere, AbbVie has sought FDA approval for Vraylar, an antipsychotic drug used in the treatment of schizophrenia and bipolar disorder, for major depressive disorder.

Between the success of Rinvoq and Skyrizi and the potential treatments progressing through the company's pipeline, AbbVie should be well-positioned to grow even without the cash-generation of its former bellwether Humira.

Parting Thoughts

I think it is safe to say that I missed a golden opportunity to buy a high-quality company with a remarkably high dividend at a bargain price back between 2019 and 2022. I do not believe that AbbVie's share price is likely to drop to levels where the stock would yield 5% or more any time in the foreseeable future. With its status as a Dividend King (thanks to its inherited history as Abbot Laboratories), its recent streak of moderate dividend hikes, and two powerhouse drugs to replace the loss of Humira's income, AbbVie will very likely continue to raise its dividend and should be able to cover it comfortably with free cash flow for years to come. The company's strategic acquisitions and in-house research have produced several promising drugs that may well supplement the revenue generated by Rinvoq and Skyrizi, paving the way for potentially greater dividend growth and share price appreciation. I rate AbbVie a buy and I am proverbially kicking myself for not buying in a few years ago.

data.title: Voyager Technologies: Not Joining On The Mission
data.description:
Shares of Voyager Technologies (NYSE:VOYG) have seen spectacular momentum in the first days of trading, as investors are jumping on the bandwagon on the back of anticipated growth in defense, space and Voyager's specific role in replacing the ISS.

These remains largely ambitions here, as the actual results show little reason to be very upbeat amidst sky-high sales multiples, modest growth and substantial losses reported, making it very easy to pass on this opportunity.

Innovation-Driven Defense & Space

Voyager Technologies describes its own business to be an innovation-driven defense technology and space solutions business. The company was purposely built to focus on providing solutions in such industries, as its founding took place as recent as 2019.

The company operates in a dual business model in which it is a prime contractor, through which it provides fulsome and integrated solutions directly to customers. On the other hand, it is a merchant supplier as well, in which it is a subcontractor to provide critical technologies to support various programs, partnering up with prime names like Palantir, NASA and Lockheed Martin.

The company reports and organizes its activities under three segments being: Defense & National Security, Space Solutions as well as Star lab Space Stations. The first segment focuses on solutions to protect dynamic and contested domains in which technology, navigation, controls, signal intelligence and defense systems are provided.

Space solutions cover solutions which deliver on space infrastructure, space technology and other services which support missions from earth to space. The Starlab Space business is a commercial space station which has been planned to succeed the ISS by 2030 in a joint venture with many parties, yet Voyager holds about two-thirds of the shares.

The latter segment is not generating revenues yet, with reported revenues split evenly between the first two segments, based on the 2024 results. Customer concentration is a risk, with NASA responsible for a quarter of sales, Lockheed Martin for about a sixth and other key clients including Airbus, Air Force, Teledyne, and some others.

Valuation and IPO Thoughts

Voyager aimed to sell 11 million shares in a preliminary price range between $26 and $29 per share, with final pricing set at $31 per share. With all shares sold by the business itself, gross proceeds from the offering came in at $341 million, excluding the over-allotment option.

With just over 55 million shares outstanding, the equity of the business is valued at $1.70 billion at the offer price. This actually includes a roughly $320 million net cash position on a pro forma basis, suggesting that operating assets are valued at around $1.38 billion at the offer price.

The company generated just over $136 million in sales in 2023 on which a GAAP operating loss of $14 million was reported. This number was a bit complicated as it included a negative R&D expense to the tune of $18 million, as otherwise operating losses came in around $32 million (although results were impacted by a more than $6 million amortization charge).

Revenues rose in a modest fashion to $144 million in 2024, yet operating losses increased to $48 million. Even if we adjust for a $3 million and change impairment loss and amortization charges increasing to $9 million and change, operating losses were on the increase.

The company reported adjusted EBITDA of minus $17 million. The first two commercial segments posted modest segment EBITDA, with overall losses due to an >$8 million Starlab Space Solutions negative EBITDA contribution and nearly $14 million in corporate overhead being allocated.

First quarter sales for 2025 rose by 14%, but only came in at $34.5 million, for a run rate just shy of $140 million. Worse is that reported operating losses jumped from nearly $12 million to just over $26 million, only in part the result of higher R&D expenses.

Shares of Voyager rose to the $55 mark in the days following the offering, pushing up the market value to $3.02 billion, with operating assets valued around $2.7 billion. This results in a very high valuation, as the business is valued at around 20 times sales, while topline sale growth is rather modest, and substantial losses are reported.

Note that the valuation has come down a long way from the peak, with shares hitting levels in the mid-seventies immediately after the offering, levels at which the firm was valued over a billion more than the valuation currently stands.

Concluding Thoughts

Given the valuation discussion above, it is obvious what the key risks are. A 20 times sales multiple, amidst lackluster (organic) growth and huge losses, makes it easy to be cautious. Since its founding in 2019, the company has pursued some seven acquisitions, making it hard to disentangle organic from M&A led growth.

Bookings for 2024 came in at $159 million, some 10% above the reported revenues that year, yet the total funded backlog of $101 million feels rather modest in relation to the current base, also given the industry in which the business operates.

Risk factors, other than valuation, are that of M&A led growth, reliance on a few big customers, fierce competition and technological changes, risks in fixed-price contracts, additional capital spending required for Starlab, concerns about DOGE, products malfunctioning, among others.

While the potential is there and the company is hyped up, I find it very hard to rhyme the current sales multiple, order intake and backlog in relation to the current valuation of Voyager, as real momentum and a buzz has been created around the IPO. In fact, shares nearly traded at three times the preliminary valuation range in the first moments after shares started trading.

Right now, this is for the believers and huge advancement has to be made before appeal can be found and fundamental support kicks in. I look forward with great interest to keeping an eye on the operating momentum which the company is poised to display. This makes it very easy for me to avoid the shares, as it is far too risky to have a short thesis on the business, but the buzz around the stock is never enough of a reason to get involved with the stock.

data.title: Time To Buy These 3 Super SWANs
data.description:
This article was co-produced with Kody Kester.

Fellow analyst and colleague, Dividend Sensei (Adam Galas), often says that it's forever and always a market of stocks and not a stock market. An adage that he picked up from FAST Graphs' Chuck Carnevale and adapted.

This isn't just a cute phrase, either.

It's based entirely on reality.

Each week, we use The Dividend Kings' Zen Research Terminal to screen our master list of 500 stocks for compelling investment ideas for our platform and Seeking Alpha.

To date, we have never had much difficulty in finding candidates to cover that fit within our investment criteria.

What's even crazier is that we're only scratching the surface of investment possibilities as they currently exist: There are tens of thousands of publicly traded companies around the world.

That's why Adam and the team are currently hard at work in the middle of a weeks-long coding marathon. This will train and automate a new and improved proprietary model that will eventually allow DK to scale the master list to 10,000 of the highest-quality businesses around the world.

20x our current master list.

In other words, that's 20x the possibilities.

When this is complete and fine-tuned, there will certainly be no shortage of compelling blue-chip value opportunities. The team even plans on launching automated company-specific reports within the next few weeks and improving them over time.

In the meantime, we will be here to provide our readers with some actionable ideas and commentary. On that note, I'll jump into our picks for the week.

U.S. Bancorp (USB): A Super-Regional Bank In The Bargain Bin

First up is U.S. Bancorp, a leading regional bank and financial services company. USB is the parent company of its primary operating entity, called U.S. Bank National Association, aka U.S. Bank. Operating almost 4,500 ATMs and more than 2,100 branches in 26 U.S. states, USB is the Midwest's largest regional bank.

As of March 31st, 2025, the company had roughly $669 billion in average assets on its balance sheet. USB's client base of 13 million consumers, 1.4 million businesses, 500K wealth clients, and 45K corporate and institutional clients is also a testament to its impressive reach.

The company's net revenue is also quite balanced.

Approximately 42% of net revenue was derived from Wealth, Corporate, Commercial & Institutional Banking activities. These include core banking, payment processing, capital markets, asset management, and brokerage services.

Another 32% came from Consumer & Business Banking activities (i.e., consumer banking, small business banking, and consumer lending).

The remaining 26% came from Payment Services activities. These include consumer and business credit cards, debit cards, and merchant processing.

Looking ahead, USB's strategic priorities should bode well for future growth. In her opening remarks during the Q1 2025 Earnings Call, new President and CEO Gunjan Kedia emphasized plans to return it to the expense discipline that has made it great in the past.

Kedia explained that the company expects to achieve positive operating leverage of more than 200 basis points for the full year 2025. Disciplined expense management and organic growth initiatives (e.g., improving momentum in fee revenue) are going to lead the way in achieving this feat.
