In the long term, continued expense management, deepening client engagement to power organic growth, and transforming the payments division can keep up growth. This is why I believe the FAST Graphs analyst consensus for 11.2% annual diluted EPS growth over the next few years is realistic.

USB's 4.7% dividend yield is also attractive, nearly quadruple of the S&P 500 index's (SP500) 1.3% yield. The payout ratio is also expected to be in the mid-40% range for 2025, which is manageable. That should give USB the flexibility to build on its 14-year dividend growth streak.

The super-regional bank is a financial fortress, too. USB enjoys an A credit rating from S&P with a stable outlook.

Despite its promising forward-looking fundamentals, the market is quite downbeat on USB.

Shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of just 9.5 (based on a 55% weight for 2025 and a 45% weight for 2026).

That's substantially less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 13.5, according to data from FAST Graphs.

Compared to the current $43 share price (as of June 13th, 2025), USB is priced at a 30% discount to our fair value estimate of $61 a share (13.5 fair value P/E ratio).

If USB meets our growth expectations and reverts to fair value, it could have a 45% cumulative upside through June 2026 (43% annualized return) and roughly 17% annual total return potential by the end of 2030 (unless otherwise hyperlinked or sourced, all info in this subhead was according to USB's Q1 2025 Investor Presentation and USB's 10-K Filing).

Principal Financial Group (PFG): An Undervalued Financial Services Company

Next up is Principal Financial Group, a major financial services company. As of March 31st, 2025, the company had $1.7 trillion in assets under administration and $718 billion in assets under management. This was spread across over 70 million customers in more than 80 countries around the world.

The company offers a variety of products to businesses, individuals, and institutional clients in three business segments.

The Retirement & Income Solutions segment offers 401k and 403b plans, annuity products, and employee stock ownership plans to businesses (mostly in the U.S.). This made up about 44% of PFG's total TTM non-GAAP pre-tax operating earnings as of March 31st.

The Principal Asset Management segment provides investment management services to institutional, retirement, and individual clients throughout the globe. This contributed another 36% to PFG's total TTM non-GAAP pre-tax operating earnings.

The Benefits and Protection segment offers insurance and group benefits in the U.S. Products and services include individual life insurance, group life/dental/vision/disability/supplemental insurance, and estate planning for high-net-worth clients. This comprised the remaining 20% of the company's total TTM non-GAAP pre-tax operating earnings.

PFG is targeting 9% to 12% annual non-GAAP operating earnings per diluted share growth. PFG has growth catalysts in the years ahead to deliver on this growth target.

That's because the company can leverage its trusted reputation in the retirement, asset management, and insurance industries to win over more customers. This can be done through deploying 15% to 25% of its net income to organic capital deployment to pursue growth opportunities as they arise.

PFG also routinely allocates between 35% and 45% of net income to return capital to shareholders via share repurchases. This consistently reduces the share count, which increases the slice of the profit pie that each shareholder is entitled to over time.

The remaining 0% to 10% of net income (besides the 40% that's allocated to dividends) is allocated to strategic acquisitions to bolster PFG's competitive positioning.

Over the next few years, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is that the company's non-GAAP operating earnings per diluted share will compound by 12% annually.

PFG provides shareholders with a 4% dividend yield, too. This is well-covered, with the payout ratio set to be in the high 30% range in 2025. That's why I believe PFG can keep delivering robust dividend growth to shareholders, extending its 16-year dividend growth streak.

Like USB, PFG is also financially vigorous. The company possesses an A- credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook.

PFG's shares are attractively valued as well. Shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of just 8.9. Per data from FAST Graphs, that's materially under the 10-year average P/E ratio of 11.3.

At the current $76 share price, PFG's shares are 21% undervalued to my $96 fair value estimate (11.3 P/E ratio).

If PFG returns to fair value and matches growth forecasts, it could have a 30% cumulative upside ahead by the end of June 2026 (29% annualized return) and 15% annual total return potential through 2030 (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead came from PFG's June 2025 Investor Presentation and PFG's 10-K Filing).

Hormel Foods (HRL): A Discounted Dividend King

The final pick of today is none other than Hormel Foods. With more than 40 of its brands commanding No. 1 or No. 2 market shares in their respective categories, HRL has many well-known brands.

These brands are sold in over 80 countries around the world, including China, the U.S., Japan, the Philippines, South Korea, and Australia. Some notable brands are Hormel pepperoni, Planters nuts, Skippy peanut butter, and Spam lunch meat.

HRL believes that it can generate 5% to 7% annual operating income growth over the long haul. Organic net sales growth of 2% to 3% annually will factor into this growth. Raising brand awareness and adding to value-added capacity will be instrumental to attaining these results.

Improving supply chain efficiency will be another piece of the growth puzzle. President and CEO Jim Snee noted in his opening remarks during the Q2 2025 Earnings Call that one of the priorities is to increase the efficiencies in its production processes.

Closing one of its three dry sausage production facilities in California and transferring this to other internal facilities was one recent move to this end.

Another was the successful opening of a new distribution center in the Memphis, Tennessee, metro area.

As a result, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is bright. Through FY 2027, analysts anticipate 8.6% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth.

HRL's 3.8% dividend yield is also appetizing. The payout ratio for FY 2025 is poised to be in the low 70% range. That should give HRL room to grow its dividend at a rate moderately less than adjusted diluted EPS growth in the coming years. This would build on an already legendary 58-year dividend growth streak.

HRL is also on a solid financial footing, with an A- credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook.

HRL's shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 18.1 (38% weight for FY 2025 and 62% weight for FY 2026). That's much less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 23.2, according to data from FAST Graphs.

From the current $31 share price, HRL is priced at a 10% discount to my $34 fair value estimate (fair value P/E ratio of 20).

If it hits growth expectations and reverts to fair value, it could have a cumulative upside of 14% through FY 2026 and 12% annual total return prospects through FY 2030 (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details in this subhead were from HRL's Q2 2025 Investor Presentation).

Risks To Consider

USB, PFG, and HRL are businesses with encouraging fundamentals. However, they still face risks that could challenge the investment thesis.

USB's risks include interest rate volatility, recession, and cyber breaches. Rising/elevated interest rates can put a dent in loan demand and raise the bank's borrowing costs.

Quick drops in interest rates could pressure USB's net interest margin if deposit costs don't also fall as quickly. A recessionary event could lead to an uptick in loan defaults on the company's loan portfolios.

Finally, major cyber breaches of USB's IT networks could compromise sensitive stakeholder data. This could tarnish the company's reputation, lead to lawsuits, and cause regulators to impose penalties on it.

Key risks to PFG include market volatility, competition, and cyber breaches. The company's asset management and retirement businesses are sensitive to fluctuations in equity and bond markets.

Market downturns could lower its assets under management and fee income. PFG's market share in retirement planning and insurance could also be hurt if it can't keep offering/launching the products that clients demand.

Lastly, the brand image could be harmed by occurrences like cyber breaches. That could trigger litigation and prompt clients to leave the company and head elsewhere.

HRL's risks include commodity price volatility, shifting consumer preferences, and supply chain disruptions. Higher costs for raw materials like turkey and pork could squeeze the company's margins if it can't pass these costs onto consumers.

If HRL can't stay ahead of the consumer preferences curve, that could also eat into its market share. Finally, labor shortages and global supply chain issues could raise the company's expenses and/or limit production capacity.

Summary: Quality Dividend Stocks With Strong Upside

USB, PFG, and HRL are each very qualitative businesses, earning 12/13 Super SWAN ratings in The Dividend Kings' Zen Research Terminal.

They're also arguably solid picks for reliable starting income.

Together, they sport an average 4.2% dividend yield.

For more perspective, that's triple the S&P 500 index's 1.3% yield.

All the while, the average payout ratio in the high-40% to low-50% range is better than the high-50% range that rating agencies like to see from their respective industries.

That should position each to build on their dividend growth streaks, ranging from 14 years for USB to 58 years for HRL.

The three also boast enviable financial strength, with A- S&P credit ratings for HRL and PFG, and an A credit rating from USB.

The icing on the cake is that the stocks are trading 10% to 30% under our fair value estimates.

If they live up to growth expectations and revert to my fair value multiples, they could deliver an average annual total return of 15% over the next five years.

Happy SWAN Investing!

data.title: Ellington Financial: Little Impact On Book Value From April Volatility Expected
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, Ellington Financial (NYSE:EFC) has outperformed the VanEck Mortgage REIT Income ETF (MORT), delivering a total return of ~12%:

Even after the recent share price gains, EFC trades at a small book value discount. What is more, the company's common stock dividend is covered by earnings, which I expect to increase in the next few years thanks to a steeper yield curve (principally driven by incremental Fed rate cuts).

In this article, I outline EFC's portfolio, recent operating performance, and the outlook for Fed policy, ultimately ranking EFC a Buy. I also discuss risks stemming from a U.S. recession, fewer-than-expected Fed rate cuts, and the likelihood of significant near-term losses during financial market turmoil.

Company Overview

You can access all EFC results on its website here. Ellington Financial is a mREIT specialized in credit investments (86% of equity, respectively 81% of assets are allocated to this strategy). The firm also operates the Longbridge platform, which originates reverse mortgages. It accounts for 12% of equity, respectively 13% of assets. Last but not least, EFC has a small agency securities portfolio where only 2% of equity and 6% of assets are allocated:

The credit portfolio is quite dynamic and varied, with management exhibiting discretion over its composition in time. As of the end of Q1 2025, the main allocation was to various types of residential mortgage loans (circa 70% of the portfolio), followed by commercial mortgages (16%):

The company's enterprise value stands at ~$16.1 billion, of which 8% is funded with common equity, 2% with preferred shares, leaving the remaining 90% for net debt.

Q1 2025 Results Overview

In Q1 2025, EFC reported adjusted distributable earnings attributable to common shareholders (a metric which strives to present earnings available for distribution, i.e., it most notably adds back realized and unrealized gains/losses) of $0.39/share, down 22% Q/Q as the prior quarter result was boosted from abnormally large unrealized losses.

On a GAAP basis, EPS stood at $0.35/share, up 40% Q/Q, benefiting from higher net interest income (which increased thanks to a lower cost of short-term funding following recent Fed rate cuts) as well as higher other income.

The company's book value stood at $13.44/share at the end of Q1 2025, down 0.6% Q/Q as EFC issued common stock during the quarter while also paying a common share dividend of $0.39/share.

Post-quarter end, the company disclosed that it estimated its book at $13.47/share at the end of April 2025, indicating the recent market volatility has had little effect on underlying performance.

Outlook for Fed policy

Futures markets price in 0.5% in Fed rate cuts by December 2025, with another 0.5% in Fed easing likely to be delivered by December 2026, ultimately taking the Fed funds rate to 3.25-3.50%:

As such, the effect of lower funding costs, which boosted performance in Q1 2025, is likely to be further amplified in the next few years. Of course, I do expect long-term interest rates to decline as well, but the net effect should be positive for EFC as I only anticipate interest rates on long-term securities to decrease by circa 0.5% from current levels, lower than the 1% decrease in the Fed funds rate by December 2026.

All in all, as the Fed makes progress with its policy normalization, we should see a steeper yield curve, which will boost performance at mREITs such as EFC, which largely borrow short-term and invest in longer-duration securities.

Company Valuation

Ellington Financial trades at a ~4% discount to its estimated April 2025 book value of $13.47/share, while the current dividend of $0.13/month remains covered by underlying earnings. As such, while the valuation is clearly not distressed, I believe the shares present a compelling investment opportunity in light of the expected boost to earnings from future Fed rate cuts.

The company is also increasing its allocation to commercial mortgages (+1% share in the portfolio relative to year-end 2024), seeking to benefit from significant opportunities in the space (as explained in my article on Claros Mortgage Trust (CMTG) here). I like management's active portfolio rotation, which will likely allow it to outperform more conservative mREITs.

To conclude, while the share price has largely recovered from the April 2025 correction, I do believe EFC will deliver attractive returns to shareholders over the next few years, ranking it a Buy.

Risks

Given EFC's overall focus on residential mortgage securities, the principal risk facing the company is a U.S. recession, which will likely be accompanied by higher unemployment and an increase in homeowner delinquencies. Indeed, the May 2025 U.S. jobs report showed a 0.2% M/M drop in the labor force participation rate, which, coupled with negative revisions of recent employment numbers, points to weak U.S. economic momentum.

Fed rate cuts may also be derailed by continuing uncertainty on trade policy or recent oil price increases. As things stand, however, the market's overall expectations align with the Fed's summary of economic projections, which put the Fed funds rate at about 3.4% at the end of 2026.

Due to the significant amount of leverage employed, mREITs such as EFC are highly exposed to financial markets volatility and a breakdown in typical correlations during times of extreme stress. For instance, the company's share price slumped by ~79% during the March 2020 COVID-19 crash.

Conclusion

Ellington Financial delivered solid Q1 2025 earnings, with the mREIT benefiting from higher net interest income and higher other income on its diversified investment portfolio.

Looking ahead, the Fed is widely expected to deliver incremental rate cuts later in 2025 and into 2026. I think that this will result in a steeper yield curve, boosting earnings at mREITs such as EFC, which borrow short-term and invest in long-duration securities.

Against this backdrop, EFC shares still trade at a small book value discount, despite the company covering its generous dividend with adjusted distributable earnings attributable to common shareholders. As such, even after the outperformance relative to the VanEck Mortgage REIT Income ETF so far in 2025, I believe a Buy rating is warranted for EFC.

Investors should consider risks stemming from a potential U.S. recession, fewer than anticipated Fed rate cuts, and significant near-term losses during times of financial markets volatility.

Thank you for reading.
